front: What is the central thesis of the "Fourth Amendment Recalibration" lens ("Fourth Amendment 2.0")?
back: The Fourth Amendment is not a fixed 1791 snapshot but a balance between government power and individual privacy that the Supreme Court must re-fit every time technology or society shifts the ground — the same constitutional text, recalibrated again and again to a changed world.

front: What are the two engines that drive Fourth Amendment recalibration in the instructor's conceptual lens?
back: (1) New surveillance and transport technology (which can expand or contract government power), and (2) incorporation against the states (the Amendment going from a federal-only restraint to a fully state-binding one).

front: In the recalibration lens, does new technology always push government power in one direction?
back: No. Technology has expanded government power (Carroll's auto exception; Olmstead's wiretap escaping the Amendment) and contracted it (Katz reclaiming the wiretap, Riley the phone, Carpenter location data). The metaphor is re-fitting the balance, not a one-way march.

front: Why does "recalibration" of the Fourth Amendment often mean overruling rather than a gentle gloss?
back: Sometimes the Court concludes the old rule simply no longer fits a changed technology and discards it. Olmstead's trespass theory was good law for roughly 40 years before Katz (1967) overruled it.

front: Which early, pre-Carroll Prohibition-era circuit decision on automobile search did the Supreme Court survey before recognizing the automobile exception?
back: Milam v. United States (4th Cir. 1924) — a Fourth Circuit decision (persuasive, not binding) on warrantless automobile search for contraband liquor, part of the pre-Carroll circuit case law in the Carroll lineage.
cite: Milam v. United States, 296 F. 629 (4th Cir. 1924)

front: What is the precedential weight of Milam v. United States, 296 F. 629 (4th Cir. 1924)?
back: Circuit-persuasive only — it is a Fourth Circuit Court of Appeals decision, not Supreme Court authority. It matters as part of the pre-Carroll circuit case law surveyed in Carroll, not as a binding rule.
cite: Milam v. United States, 296 F. 629 (4th Cir. 1924)

front: What did Carroll v. United States (1925) hold (the automobile exception)?
back: With probable cause, a movable vehicle may be searched without a warrant because its mobility makes obtaining one impracticable — the Fourth Amendment re-fitted to the automobile and Prohibition enforcement.

front: In the recalibration arc, which direction did Carroll v. United States (1925) push government power, and why?
back: It expanded government power: the new transport technology (the automobile) justified warrantless searches, dispensing with the warrant requirement for movable vehicles.

front: Common pitfall: does Carroll's automobile exception let officers search any vehicle on a hunch?
back: No. The automobile exception still requires probable cause — it dispenses with the warrant, not with the cause. It is not a suspicion-free license to search any vehicle.

front: What did United States v. Lee (1927) hold about a Coast Guard officer's searchlight?
back: Using a searchlight to illuminate a boat's deck on the high seas was not a search — it revealed only what was in open view. An early sense-enhancement-is-not-a-search case.

front: Why does United States v. Lee (1927) matter in the recalibration arc — what doctrinal line does it seed?
back: It is the seed of the sense-enhancement line that later flowers in Kyllo: it raises whether a device merely improves ordinary perception (open view, not a search) or pierces a protected space.

front: What single recalibration principle connects United States v. Lee (1927) to Kyllo (2001)?
back: Does the device merely improve ordinary perception, or does it pierce a space the Amendment protects? A searchlight on open view was not a search (Lee); a thermal imager probing through a home's wall with technology not in general public use is a search (Kyllo).

front: What did Olmstead v. United States (1928) hold about wiretapping?
back: Wiretapping that involved no physical trespass into the premises and seized no tangible thing was not a Fourth Amendment search or seizure — the property/trespass theory of the Amendment (wiretap = no trespass = no search).

front: Common pitfall: is Olmstead v. United States (1928) still good law for the rule that a wiretap is not a search?
back: No. Olmstead governed for about 40 years but was overruled by Katz (1967). Quoting Olmstead for the proposition that a wiretap is not a search states the former rule.

front: In the recalibration arc, which direction did Olmstead v. United States (1928) push government power?
back: It expanded government power: the new technology (the telephone wiretap) escaped the Fourth Amendment entirely under the trespass theory, leaving the surveillance unregulated.

front: Whose Olmstead opinion foreshadowed the later privacy turn the Fourth Amendment would take?
back: Justice Brandeis's dissent in Olmstead (1928) foreshadowed the privacy approach that Katz (1967) would later adopt.

front: What did Wolf v. Colorado (1949) hold (incorporation, step one)?
back: The substance/core of the Fourth Amendment binds the states through the Fourteenth Amendment's Due Process Clause — but it expressly declined to impose the federal exclusionary remedy on the states (states could choose their own).

front: What did Mapp v. Ohio (1961) hold (incorporation, step two)?
back: The Fourth Amendment exclusionary rule applies to the states through the Fourteenth Amendment — evidence from an unconstitutional search or seizure is inadmissible in state court. It overruled Wolf on the remedy and completed incorporation.

front: Distinguish Wolf v. Colorado (1949) from Mapp v. Ohio (1961) in the incorporation two-step — exactly what did each incorporate against the states?
back: Wolf (1949) incorporated the RIGHT — the substance of the Fourth Amendment — but imposed no federal exclusionary remedy. Mapp (1961) incorporated the REMEDY — the exclusionary rule — overruling Wolf on that point. So: 1949 = the right; 1961 = the remedy. Treating either case as doing both is a common error.

front: Common pitfall: what is wrong with saying "until 1949 the Fourth Amendment only applied to the federal government, and then it applied to the states" as a single event?
back: It collapses twelve years of doctrine. 1949 (Wolf) incorporated only the right against the states; the exclusionary remedy did not reach the states until 1961 (Mapp). Stating it as one event omits the Wolf-to-Mapp two-step and overruling.

front: How does the incorporation engine (Wolf → Mapp) differ from the technology engine in the recalibration lens?
back: It runs on its own axis: instead of re-fitting the Amendment to a new device, it extends the Amendment from a federal-only restraint to a fully state-binding one — first the right (Wolf 1949), then the remedy (Mapp 1961).

front: What did Katz v. United States (1967) hold, and what famous phrase captures it?
back: The Fourth Amendment "protects people, not places"; a search occurs when the government violates a person's reasonable expectation of privacy. It overruled Olmstead's trespass requirement.

front: In Katz v. United States (1967), whose opinion supplies the two-part reasonable-expectation-of-privacy (REP) test?
back: Justice Harlan's concurrence supplies the two-part reasonable-expectation-of-privacy test.
cite: Katz v. United States, 389 U.S. 347, 361 (1967) (Harlan, J., concurring)

front: Describe the Olmstead → Katz recalibration: what theory of "search" replaced what, and by what mechanism?
back: Recalibration by overruling: Katz (1967) replaced Olmstead's property/trespass theory (a search requires physical intrusion) with the privacy theory (a search occurs when the government invades a reasonable expectation of privacy). The same text, re-fitted from places to people.

front: Electronic eavesdropping on a public phone-booth call, with no physical entry into the booth, was held a Fourth Amendment search — which case?
back: Katz v. United States (1967) — the absence of trespass did not defeat the search, because the Amendment protects a reasonable expectation of privacy, not just physical spaces.

front: In the recalibration arc, what is the one-line significance of Kyllo v. United States (2001)? (Full treatment lives on the digital-surveillance page.)
back: Thermal imaging of a home with technology not in general public use is a search — the modern recalibration of the sense-enhancement principle running from United States v. Lee.

front: In the recalibration arc, what is the one-line significance of Riley v. California (2014)?
back: Police generally may not search a cell phone's data incident to arrest without a warrant — the search-incident-to-arrest exception does not reach the vast trove of data on a modern smartphone.

front: Common pitfall: what is the correct merits citation for Riley v. California (2014), and what must you avoid grabbing?
back: Cite the merits opinion at 573 U.S. 373 (2014) (parallel 134 S. Ct. 2473). Do not cite the cert-stage orders (134 S. Ct. 999; 134 S. Ct. 1870) — those are different clusters, not the holding.

front: In the recalibration arc, what is the one-line significance of Carpenter v. United States (2018)? (Full treatment lives on the digital-surveillance page.)
back: Acquiring cell-site location information (CSLI) is a search; the third-party doctrine does not extend to comprehensive digital location records — the current endpoint of the recalibration arc.

front: On this conceptual recalibration page, how should Kyllo, Riley, and Carpenter be treated?
back: As the modern beats of the arc — one-liners and cross-links, not full holdings. Their full doctrine lives on "The Third-Party Doctrine and Digital Surveillance" page; don't re-litigate them in full here.

front: Recalibration timeline (technology + incorporation): Milam (1924) → Carroll (1925) → Lee (1927) → {{c1::Olmstead}} (1928) → Wolf (1949) → Mapp (1961) → {{c2::Katz}} (1967) → Kyllo (2001) → Riley (2014) → Carpenter (2018).
back: c1 = Olmstead (wiretap = no trespass = no search); c2 = Katz (privacy / reasonable expectation of privacy), which overruled Olmstead.
